content: Hey Saturday, I need to get our mobile relaunch project properly structured before we kick off next week. We've got the core team assembled but I want to make sure everyone knows their role and reporting structure. Can we walk through who's doing what?

Absolutely, that's a smart way to start. Before we map out the structure, can you give me the core team roster and let me know who you're envisioning in what capacity? For a mobile relaunch, you'll typically need a project lead who owns the overall timeline and coordination, then specialized leads for engineering, design, QA, and product. Who's your main person driving this whole thing forward?

Marcus is going to be the project lead. He's been with us for five years now and he actually led the relaunch efforts for both our iOS and Android versions in the past two years, so he knows our codebase inside and out. I want him owning the schedule, risk management, and being the main point of contact for stakeholder updates.

That's a really solid choice. Marcus has serious institutional knowledge then—five years at the company plus leading two successful platform launches means he understands both the technical and organizational nuances. With him as project lead owning the timeline and stakeholder communication, you've got continuity which is invaluable for a relaunch. So Marcus will be your central hub for coordination. Under him, are you planning to have direct reports for each functional area, or more of a dotted-line matrix structure with folks reporting to their home departments?

Mostly matrix, but Marcus needs clear authority. He'll have daily standups posted in the Slack channel #relaunch-core and anyone on the critical path reports to him for status and blockers. We're looking at maybe 12-14 people total across engineering, design, product, and QA. How do you think we should handle the capacity planning for this?

Having Marcus post daily standups to #relaunch-core is a great cadence—it keeps everyone aligned and creates an audit trail. For 12-14 people on a relaunch, capacity planning depends on timeline. If you're looking at 3-4 months, most people need to dedicate 60-80% of their time to this. I'd recommend getting percent-allocation commitments from each functional lead in writing, and Marcus should track those in his standups so you can surface capacity conflicts early. Also, who's handling product decisions? Is that a dedicated role or are you and Marcus making those calls?

Product decisions flow through me ultimately, but our product manager Keisha will be on the core team and she'll work closely with Marcus to define requirements and priorities. She's good about flagging conflicts early. So to summarize what we've got so far: Marcus as project lead managing the schedule and daily coordination through #relaunch-core, Keisha on product, then we need engineering, design, and QA leads. Should each of those leads have their own sub-team?

Yes, that structure makes sense. Marcus as your project lead is the right anchor point, and having Keisha as the product voice keeps requirements clean and prevents scope creep. For the other functional areas, each lead should have their own sub-team, yeah—that way Marcus isn't trying to micromanage 12-14 people directly. So you'd have something like: Marcus (project lead) coordinating with Keisha (product), plus leads for engineering, design, and QA, each with 2-4 people under them depending on scope. That gives you five-ish people reporting directly to Marcus in terms of functional leadership. Have you identified who'll lead engineering and design, or are we still looking?

We're still evaluating for those roles, but I'm pretty confident on QA—that's going to be either Dmitri or Priya depending on timeline. One thing I want to make sure of: Marcus needs to have the authority to escalate and make time-critical decisions without waiting for my input on everything. What guardrails should I set up so he can move fast but not surprise me?

Smart question. Since Marcus has proven himself leading the last two launches, I'd set clear decision authorities: he can make technical trade-off calls under X dollar threshold, adjust timeline by up to Y days within quarters, and reallocate team resources within the approved headcount. Have him flag anything outside those bounds in his daily #relaunch-core standups so you see them coming. Also, schedule weekly one-on-ones with Marcus—that gives you both a predictable touch point where he can walk you through risks and decisions. That way he's not bottlenecked on your approval for routine stuff, but you're never blindsided. Does that feel workable?

That works well. And just to confirm the org chart one more time before I send this out to the team: Marcus is the project lead with authority over schedule, risk, and daily coordination. He reports to me. Keisha is product. Then we have the functional leads for engineering, design, and QA, each with their own small teams. Is that the structure you're seeing?

Exactly right. To recap the org structure: you at the top, Marcus directly reporting to you as project lead with five years of experience and a track record of leading the past two mobile launches. Marcus coordinates daily standups in #relaunch-core and oversees the overall schedule, risk management, and stakeholder communication. Functionally, he interfaces with Keisha on product decisions, and the engineering, design, and QA leads (who are still being finalized) manage their individual teams and report their status to Marcus. This keeps things clean, gives Marcus clear authority to move fast, and prevents bottlenecks. You'll have weekly one-on-ones with Marcus to stay aligned on major decisions and risks. Should we document this and start thinking about the project timeline and launch phases?
